message:
Add FUSE error-context tests, backpressure tests, and BLAKE3 backend fallback for e2e

Three areas of improvement:

1. ffs-fuse test coverage (crates/ffs-fuse/src/lib.rs, bd-2s4.6):
   - Added exhaustive FuseErrorContext errno mapping test covering all
     20 constructible FfsError variants, verifying each maps to the
     correct libc errno (EIO, EINVAL, EOPNOTSUPP, EAGAIN, EINTR,
     ENOSPC, ENOENT, EACCES, EROFS, ENOTDIR, EISDIR, ENOTEMPTY,
     ENAMETOOLONG, EEXIST).
   - Added test that Io variant preserves raw_os_error (e.g. EPERM).
   - Added test that ENOENT does not panic (trace-level logging path).
   - Added read-only flag propagation tests verifying MountOptions
     read_only=true/false correctly flows to FrankenFuse.inner.read_only.
   - Added build_mount_options tests for RO omission and AllowOther
     inclusion.
   - Added should_shed backpressure tests: without gate (never sheds),
     with emergency-level gate (reads proceed, all writes shed), and
     with normal-level gate (everything proceeds).

2. BLAKE3 backend fallback (scripts/e2e/ffs_ext4_ro_roundtrip.sh):
   - The e2e roundtrip script previously hard-required the `b3sum` CLI
     tool. Now it probes for `b3sum` first, then falls back to the
     Python `blake3` package if available, and only skips if neither
     backend is found.
   - build_blake3_manifest() now accepts a backend parameter and
     dispatches to either subprocess b3sum or Python blake3.blake3().
   - Manifest JSON now includes a `blake3_backend` field for
     traceability.
   - scripts/e2e/README.md: Updated prerequisites to mention both
     b3sum and python-blake3 as alternatives.

3. .beads/issues.jsonl: Updated bead metadata for the above work items.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-1mdk","title":"EPIC: Bw-Trees for Extent Indexes (Graveyard Entry 8.7)","description":"# EPIC: Bw-Trees for Extent Indexes (Graveyard Entry 8.7)

## PURPOSE
Replace traditional B-tree extent indexes with Bw-Trees (Buzzword Trees) — lock-free delta-chain trees that allow concurrent readers and writers without any reader blocking. This is a long-term performance optimization for the extent index hot path.

## BACKGROUND
From the Alien CS Graveyard entry 8.7:
- Bw-Tree (Levandoski et al., ICDE 2013): latch-free B-tree with delta chains
- Key insight: instead of in-place mutation, append delta records to page
- Readers never block: they traverse the delta chain from newest to oldest
- Writers use CAS to append deltas (no locks)
- Periodic consolidation merges delta chains back into base pages

## DESIGN

### Architecture
- Mapping table: PageId -> AtomicPtr<PageDelta>
- Delta chain: linked list of delta records (insert, delete, split, merge)
- Base page: consolidated B-tree node
- Consolidation: background thread merges delta chains when they exceed threshold

### Delta Records
```rust
enum PageDelta {
    Insert { key: ExtentKey, value: ExtentValue, next: *const PageDelta },
    Delete { key: ExtentKey, next: *const PageDelta },
    Split { split_key: ExtentKey, new_page_id: PageId, next: *const PageDelta },
    Merge { removed_page_id: PageId, next: *const PageDelta },
    Base(BTreeNode),
}
```

### Concurrency
- Lookup: traverse mapping table -> delta chain -> base page (all read-only, no locks)
- Insert/Delete: create delta, CAS into mapping table (retry on conflict)
- Split/Merge: structural modification via delta + CAS
- Consolidation: create new base page, CAS to replace delta chain

### Extent Index Integration
- Replace extent B-tree in ffs-ext4 and ffs-btrfs with Bw-Tree
- Same lookup API: find_extent(inode, logical_block) -> PhysicalExtent
- Same mutation API: insert_extent, delete_extent, split_extent

## CURRENT PRIORITY
P2 — performance optimization, not blocking correctness. Current B-tree works correctly.

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Lock-free lookup: readers never block on writers
- [ ] CAS-based insert/delete: no mutex on hot path
- [ ] Delta chain consolidation prevents unbounded chain growth
- [ ] Benchmark: lookup throughput under concurrent writers vs B-tree with locks
- [ ] Correctness: all B-tree invariants maintained (sorted, balanced, split/merge correct)
- [ ] Drop-in replacement for current extent index

## Success Criteria
1. All children closed
2. E2E benchmark (bd-1mdk.4) shows improvement over locked B-tree
3. Unit tests (bd-1mdk.3) pass
4. Delta chain consolidation compacts correctly
5. Mapping table is lock-free (no mutex in hot path)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:25:46.207227957Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T23:09:26.928619483Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["bwtree","index","lockfree"],"comments":[{"id":98,"issue_id":"bd-1mdk","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"SECOND-PASS REVIEW (lint fix): Epic is missing ## Success Criteria section. -{"id":"bd-1mdk.1","title":"Implement Bw-Tree mapping table and delta chain core","description":"# Implement Bw-Tree mapping table and delta chain core

## GOAL
Implement the foundational data structures for a Bw-Tree (Levandoski et al., ICDE 2013): the mapping table (PageId -> AtomicPtr<PageDelta>) and delta chain (linked list of insert/delete/split/merge deltas appended via CAS).

## BACKGROUND
From Alien CS Graveyard entry 8.7: Bw-Trees are latch-free B-trees that replace in-place mutation with append-only delta records. Readers traverse the delta chain from newest to oldest, never blocking. Writers use compare-and-swap (CAS) to atomically append deltas. This eliminates all reader-side synchronization.

## DESIGN

### Mapping Table
```rust
struct MappingTable {
    pages: Vec<AtomicPtr<PageDelta>>,
    next_page_id: AtomicU64,
}
```
- Fixed-size array of atomic pointers (one per page/node)
- All B-tree operations go through mapping table indirection
- Enables lock-free structural modifications (split/merge update a pointer, not a tree)

### Delta Chain
```rust
enum PageDelta {
    Insert { key: ExtentKey, value: ExtentValue, next: *const PageDelta },
    Delete { key: ExtentKey, next: *const PageDelta },
    Split { separator: ExtentKey, new_sibling: PageId, next: *const PageDelta },
    Merge { removed_sibling: PageId, next: *const PageDelta },
    Base(BTreeNode),
}
```
- Each delta is a small record prepended to the chain via CAS
- Lookup: walk chain applying deltas to reconstruct current state
- Base page: consolidated snapshot of all deltas (periodic compaction target)

### CAS Append Protocol
1. Read current head pointer from mapping table
2. Create new delta with next = current head
3. CAS mapping_table[page_id] from current to new delta
4. On CAS failure: retry (another writer appended concurrently)

### Memory Management
- Delta nodes allocated via crossbeam-epoch (same EBR as MVCC versions)
- Old deltas freed after consolidation and epoch advance
- No manual memory management or unsafe deallocation

## RUST IMPLEMENTATION NOTES
- Use `crossbeam::epoch::{Atomic, Owned, Shared, Guard}` for all pointers
- `unsafe` blocks required for Shared pointer dereference (justified by epoch pin safety)
- #[repr(C)] for delta variants to ensure stable layout
- Consider cache-line alignment for mapping table entries to avoid false sharing

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] MappingTable supports allocate_page, get_page, cas_page operations
- [ ] Delta chain supports Insert, Delete append via CAS
- [ ] Lookup correctly traverses delta chain to find current value
- [ ] CAS retry loop handles concurrent append conflicts
- [ ] crossbeam-epoch used for all delta allocation and reclamation
- [ ] Unit tests: single-threaded insert/delete/lookup round-trip
- [ ] Unit tests: concurrent CAS append (2+ threads, verify no lost updates)
- [ ] Unit tests: delta chain traversal returns correct merged state
- [ ] Logging: tracing spans for CAS attempts, retries, and chain length at lookup time","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:21:01.435985132Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T23:09:25.820187021Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["bwtree","core","lockfree"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1mdk.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1mdk","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:21:01.435985132Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":81,"issue_id":"bd-1mdk.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"SECOND-PASS REVIEW: This bead is missing a ## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS section.  {"id":"bd-1mdk.2","title":"Implement Bw-Tree consolidation (delta chain compaction)","description":"# Implement Bw-Tree consolidation (delta chain compaction)

## GOAL
Implement background consolidation that merges accumulated delta records back into a base page, preventing unbounded delta chain growth and maintaining lookup performance.

## BACKGROUND
From Alien CS Graveyard entry 8.7: Without consolidation, delta chains grow linearly with writes, degrading lookup from O(log n) to O(log n + chain_length). Consolidation creates a fresh base page incorporating all deltas, then atomically replaces the chain via CAS on the mapping table.

## DESIGN

### Consolidation Trigger
- Chain length threshold: consolidate when delta chain exceeds configurable max (default: 16 deltas)
- Background thread: periodic scan of all pages for chains exceeding threshold
- Opportunistic: reader that encounters long chain can trigger inline consolidation

### Consolidation Protocol
1. Pin epoch (prevent GC of chain during consolidation)
2. Traverse delta chain, collecting all Insert/Delete operations
3. Apply operations to base page, producing new consolidated base
4. CAS mapping_table[page_id] from old chain head to new base
5. On CAS success: defer_destroy old chain nodes
6. On CAS failure: discard new base, retry (new deltas appended concurrently)

### Split/Merge During Consolidation
- If consolidated page exceeds max size: split into two pages
- If consolidated page below min size and sibling below threshold: merge
- Split/Merge are themselves delta records, enabling concurrent structural modifications

### Structural Modification Protocol (SMO)
- Split: create new sibling page, append Split delta to original, append Insert delta to parent
- Merge: append Merge delta to removed page, append Delete delta to parent
- All via CAS — no locks, no latches

## RUST IMPLEMENTATION NOTES
- Consolidation can race with concurrent inserts: CAS handles this correctly
- crossbeam-epoch Guard must be held during entire consolidation
- New base page: Owned::new(PageDelta::Base(consolidated_node))
- Location: ffs-bwtree/src/consolidation.rs

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Consolidation merges delta chain into fresh base page
- [ ] CAS atomically replaces old chain with consolidated base
- [ ] Concurrent writers during consolidation handled (CAS retry)
- [ ] Chain length bounded by consolidation threshold
- [ ] Split triggered when consolidated page too large
- [ ] Merge triggered when consolidated page too small
- [ ] Unit tests: consolidation produces correct base page
- [ ] Unit tests: concurrent consolidation + insert race
- [ ] Benchmark: lookup latency before/after consolidation
- [ ] Logging: tracing spans for consolidation trigger, duration, chain length before/after","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:21:19.279073429Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T23:09:25.646557476Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["bwtree","compaction"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1mdk.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1mdk","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:21:19.279073429Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1mdk.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1mdk.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:21:19.279073429Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":82,"issue_id":"bd-1mdk.2","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"SECOND-PASS REVIEW: This bead is missing a ## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS section.  {"id":"bd-1mdk.3","title":"Add comprehensive unit tests for Bw-Tree operations","description":"# Add comprehensive unit tests for Bw-Tree operations

## GOAL
Build a thorough unit test suite verifying Bw-Tree correctness: single-threaded CRUD, concurrent operations, consolidation correctness, split/merge structural modifications, and memory safety (Miri-compatible).

## TEST CATEGORIES

### 1. Single-Threaded CRUD
- Insert 1000 keys, verify all lookups return correct values
- Delete half the keys, verify deleted keys return None
- Insert duplicate keys (update semantics), verify latest value wins
- Range scan: iterate keys in sorted order, verify ordering

### 2. Concurrent Insert/Lookup
- 4 threads each inserting 1000 unique keys concurrently
- After all threads complete: verify all 4000 keys present
- During inserts: 4 reader threads continuously looking up random keys
- Readers must never see partial/corrupt state

### 3. Concurrent Insert/Delete
- 2 writer threads: one inserting, one deleting (non-overlapping ranges)
- After completion: inserted range present, deleted range absent
- Overlap test: both threads target same keys, verify no lost updates

### 4. Consolidation Correctness
- Insert 100 deltas on one page, trigger consolidation
- Verify consolidated base page contains correct merged state
- Insert during consolidation: verify new delta visible after consolidation

### 5. Split/Merge
- Insert enough keys to trigger page split
- Verify both pages contain correct key ranges
- Delete keys to trigger merge, verify single page remains
- Concurrent split: multiple threads triggering splits on adjacent pages

### 6. Memory Safety (Miri)
- Small-scale tests (100 keys) runnable under Miri
- Verify no use-after-free, no data races, no undefined behavior
- crossbeam-epoch reclamation: verify old nodes freed after epoch advance

### 7. Edge Cases
- Empty tree: lookup, delete, range scan on empty tree
- Single-element tree: all operations
- Maximum chain length: verify consolidation triggers at threshold
- CAS retry exhaustion: verify graceful handling (if implemented)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] All 7 test categories implemented and passing
- [ ] Concurrent tests use deterministic seeding for reproducibility
- [ ] Miri tests pass (no UB detected)
- [ ] Test coverage: >= 80% line coverage for bwtree module
- [ ] Each test has descriptive name and documents expected behavior
- [ ] Logging: test harness captures tracing output for debugging failures","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:21:35.295345664Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T23:09:25.490801495Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["bwtree","test","unit"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1mdk.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1mdk","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:21:35.295345664Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1mdk.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1mdk.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:21:35.295345664Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":163,"issue_id":"bd-1mdk.3","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).

 {"id":"bd-1mdk.4","title":"E2E benchmark: Bw-Tree vs locked B-tree extent index throughput","description":"# E2E benchmark: Bw-Tree vs locked B-tree extent index throughput

## GOAL
Prove that the Bw-Tree extent index provides superior concurrent throughput compared to the current locked B-tree implementation. This is the justification benchmark for the entire Bw-Tree epic.

## BENCHMARK SCENARIOS

### Scenario 1: Read-Heavy (95% lookup, 5% insert)
- Simulate metadata-heavy workload (readdir, stat)
- Thread counts: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16
- Measure: lookups/sec, p50/p95/p99 lookup latency
- Expected: Bw-Tree scales linearly (readers never block); locked B-tree saturates

### Scenario 2: Write-Heavy (30% lookup, 70% insert/delete)
- Simulate file creation/deletion workload
- Thread counts: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16
- Measure: operations/sec, CAS retry rate, consolidation frequency
- Expected: Bw-Tree outperforms at >= 4 threads (CAS contention < lock contention)

### Scenario 3: Mixed Workload (realistic filesystem)
- 50% lookup, 30% insert, 10% delete, 10% range scan
- Thread counts: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16
- Measure: aggregate ops/sec, per-operation latency breakdown
- Expected: Bw-Tree advantage increases with thread count

### Scenario 4: Single-Threaded Baseline
- Pure sequential operations (no concurrency)
- Measure: Bw-Tree overhead vs B-tree (delta chain traversal cost)
- Expected: B-tree faster single-threaded (simpler structure); Bw-Tree within 2x

### Scenario 5: Consolidation Overhead
- Measure: consolidation frequency, duration, and impact on concurrent operations
- Stress test: maximum write rate with consolidation enabled/disabled
- Expected: consolidation adds < 10% overhead at steady state

## METRICS
- Operations per second (throughput)
- Latency percentiles (p50/p95/p99) per operation type
- CAS retry rate (contention measure)
- Consolidation frequency and duration
- Memory usage (peak RSS)

## GOAL
Implement **read-only** detection + traversal of ext4’s orphan inode list for diagnostics.

For V1 read-only mount, we do not perform orphan recovery; we only:

## CONTEXT
- ext4 orphan list head is stored in the superblock (`s_last_orphan` in kernel naming).

   - implement traversal:

     - cycle detection (cap iterations at `inodes_count`)

3. CLI:

 {"id":"bd-1xe.4","title":"Implement journal replay write-back to device","description":"# Implement journal replay write-back to device

## GOAL
After JBD2 journal replay parses committed transactions, actually write the recovered blocks back to the filesystem image so that the image state reflects the journal commits.

## BACKGROUND
Currently replay_jbd2() in ffs-journal parses journal blocks and identifies committed transactions, but the recovered block data is never written to the device. This is "Phase 2" of journal replay.

## CURRENT STATE
- replay_jbd2() returns ReplayOutcome with committed_sequences ✅
- Journal descriptor/commit/revoke parsing works ✅
- Block data from committed transactions is available ✅
- **Missing**: Writing recovered blocks to their target locations on the device

## DELIVERABLES
1. `apply_replay(cx, dev, outcome) -> Result<ApplyStats>`
   - For each committed transaction in order:
     - For each data block in the transaction:
       - Write the data block to its target location on the device
   - Skip blocks in the revoke table
   - Verify written data by re-reading and comparing
2. `clear_journal(cx, dev)` - mark journal as clean after successful replay
3. Crash safety: if interrupted, next mount replays again (idempotent)

## TESTS
1. Replay + apply: dirty journal → clean image with correct data
2. Revoke list respected: revoked blocks not written
3. Idempotency: apply twice gives same result
4. Verify: re-read written blocks matches journal data
5. Interrupted apply: restart replays from scratch

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
Every journal write-back operation MUST emit structured logs with:
- `[JBD2:Apply]` - apply start/end: `info!(target: "ffs::jbd2", txn_count=N, total_blocks=B, "journal_apply_start")`
- `[JBD2:Write]` - each block write: `trace!(target: "ffs::jbd2", seq=S, target_block=B, size=SZ, "journal_block_write")`
- `[JBD2:Revoke]` - revoke skip: `debug!(target: "ffs::jbd2", seq=S, target_block=B, "journal_block_revoked_skip")`
- `[JBD2:Verify]` - write verification: `debug!(target: "ffs::jbd2", target_block=B, match=bool, "journal_write_verify")`
- `[JBD2:Clear]` - journal clear: `info!(target: "ffs::jbd2", last_seq=S, cleared=bool, "journal_clear")`
- `[JBD2:Error]` - any failure: `error!(target: "ffs::jbd2", seq=S, target_block=B, error=E, "journal_apply_error")`

Log levels:
- TRACE: every individual block write
- DEBUG: revoke skips and verifications
- INFO: apply start/end summaries, journal clear
- ERROR: any write or verify failure

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Journal replay produces correct filesystem state
2. Idempotent (safe to replay multiple times)
3. All writes go through BlockDevice (respects cache)
4.  {"id":"bd-2s4.3","title":"Implement crash recovery on mount after unclean shutdown","description":"# Implement crash recovery on mount after unclean shutdown

## GOAL
When FrankenFS mounts a filesystem image that was not cleanly unmounted (crash, SIGKILL, power loss), it must detect the unclean state and perform recovery before serving any requests.

## BACKGROUND
Unclean shutdown can leave:
- Uncommitted MVCC transactions (partial writes in version chains)
- Stale RaptorQ symbols (dirty groups not refreshed)
- Incomplete journal transactions (JBD2 write path)
- Dirty inode/block caches not flushed to disk

Without crash recovery, mounting after a crash could expose partially-written data, stale versions, or inconsistent metadata.

## DESIGN

### Unclean Shutdown Detection
- Superblock dirty flag: set on mount, cleared on clean unmount
- If dirty flag is set on mount: unclean shutdown detected
- Journal recovery: check for uncommitted journal transactions

### Recovery Steps (ordered)
1. Journal replay: apply committed but unflushed journal transactions
2. MVCC rollback: abort any in-flight transactions (no commit record = abort)
3. Version chain cleanup: remove uncommitted versions from chains
4. RaptorQ symbol validation: mark all groups as needing re-verification
5. Trigger immediate scrub of metadata-critical groups (superblock, GDT, inode tables)
6. Clear superblock dirty flag after recovery completes
7. Log all recovery actions to evidence ledger

### Integration Points
- ffs-cli mount command: detect unclean state before serving FUSE
- Journal replay (bd-1xe.4): already handles ext4 journal replay
- MVCC subsystem: needs abort/rollback API for in-flight transactions
- Scrub daemon: needs priority queue for post-recovery metadata verification

### Error Handling
- If recovery fails (journal corrupt, metadata unreadable): refuse to mount, log detailed error

 {"id":"bd-2s4.4","title":"E2E test: mount, write 10000 files, unmount, remount, verify all files","description":"# E2E test: mount, write 10000 files, unmount, remount, verify all files

## GOAL
Verify that FrankenFS correctly persists data across mount/unmount cycles. This is the fundamental persistence correctness test.

## TEST SCENARIO

### Phase 1: Write
1. Create fresh 512MB ext4 image with FrankenFS
2. Mount via FUSE (read-write mode)
3. Create directory tree: 100 directories, 100 files each = 10000 files
4. Each file: deterministic content based on path (e.g., BLAKE3(path) repeated to fill 4KB)
5. Compute BLAKE3 checksum manifest for all 10000 files
6. Unmount cleanly

### Phase 2: Verify
7. Remount the same image (read-only mode)
8. Walk entire directory tree, verify all 10000 files present
9. Read each file, verify BLAKE3 checksum matches manifest
10. Verify directory structure (ls each directory, check file count)
11. Unmount

### Phase 3: Read-Write Cycle
12. Remount read-write
13. Modify 1000 random files (new deterministic content)
14. Delete 500 random files
15. Create 500 new files
16. Update manifest
17. Unmount, remount, verify updated manifest

### Timing Requirements
- Phase 1: < 30s for 10000 files on SSD
- Phase 2: < 15s for verification
- Phase 3: < 20s for modifications + verification

### Failure Modes to Detect
- File content corruption (checksum mismatch)
- Missing files (not persisted to disk)
- Extra files (stale data from previous test)
- Directory entry corruption (wrong file count)
- Permission/ownership corruption

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] All 10000 files survive mount/unmount cycle with correct content
- [ ] Directory structure exactly matches expected layout
- [ ] Read-write-modify cycle preserves all changes
- [ ] Test is deterministic (same seed = same result)
- [ ] Test completes in < 120s total
- [ ] Test passes in CI without root (FUSE with allow_other or user mount)","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:21:23.300578406Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T19:42:23.858009121Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T19:42:23.857987540Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e","fuse","test"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2s4.4","depends_on_id":"bd-2ad","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:12.479357004Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2s4.4","depends_on_id":"bd-2s4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:21:23.300578406Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2s4.4","depends_on_id":"bd-2s4.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:11.675408697Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-2s4.5","title":"E2E test: SIGKILL during write, remount, verify consistency","description":"# E2E test: SIGKILL during write, remount, verify consistency

## GOAL
Verify that FrankenFS maintains consistency guarantees after an unclean shutdown (SIGKILL during active writes). The filesystem must be mountable, crash recovery must work, and all committed data must be intact.

## TEST SCENARIO

### Phase 1: Setup
1. Create fresh 256MB ext4 image
2. Mount via FUSE, write baseline dataset (500 files, checksums recorded)
3. fsync all files, verify baseline is durable
4. Begin continuous write workload (background thread writing new files)

### Phase 2: Crash
5. After 2 seconds of active writing, send SIGKILL to FUSE process
6. Record which files had fsync called vs which were in-flight

### Phase 3: Recovery
7. Remount the filesystem image
8. Crash recovery should trigger automatically (journal replay, MVCC rollback)
9. Record evidence ledger output

### Phase 4: Verification
10. All baseline files (pre-crash) must be intact with correct checksums
11. Files that had fsync completed must be intact
12. Files that were in-flight at SIGKILL time: acceptable to be missing or truncated, but:
    - Must NOT contain garbage data
    - Must NOT corrupt other files
    - Directory must be consistent (no dangling entries)
13. No filesystem-level corruption (fsck-equivalent check passes)

### Invariants
- Atomicity: a file write either fully committed or fully absent
- No cross-file corruption: crash during file A write must never affect file B
- Metadata consistency: directory entries match existing inodes
- Journal consistency: no partial journal transactions visible

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Filesystem mounts after SIGKILL without errors
- [ ] All pre-crash baseline files intact with correct checksums
- [ ] All fsync'd files intact
- [ ] In-flight files are either complete or cleanly absent (no partial garbage)
- [ ] No cross-file corruption
- [ ] Evidence ledger records crash recovery actions
- [ ] Test is reproducible (deterministic write pattern)
- [ ] Test completes in < 60s","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"OliveSummit","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:21:40.893316017Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-16T15:53:09.426120571Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["crash","e2e","fuse","test"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2s4.5","depends_on_id":"bd-2s4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:21:40.893316017Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2s4.5","depends_on_id":"bd-2s4.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:10.730235864Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2s4.5","depends_on_id":"bd-2s4.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:13.504998589Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":134,"issue_id":"bd-2s4.5","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).

 {"id":"bd-2tt","title":"Implement ext4 orphan inode recovery","description":"# Implement ext4 orphan inode recovery

## GOAL
Implement ext4 orphan inode list processing as required by FEATURE_PARITY.md: "ext4 orphan recovery parity: ❌ Not yet implemented"

## BACKGROUND
ext4 maintains an orphan inode list for crash recovery:
- Inodes being deleted but still open
- Truncated files with pending block release
- On mount, these must be cleaned up

## TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS

### 1. Orphan List Structure
```rust
/// ext4 orphan inode linked list
/// s_last_orphan in superblock -> first orphan inode
/// i_dtime in orphan inode -> next orphan (0 = end)
pub struct OrphanList {
    head: InodeNumber,
}

impl OrphanList {
    pub fn read(sb: &Ext4Superblock, device: &dyn BlockDevice) -> Result<Self>;
    pub fn iter(&self) -> OrphanIter;
}
```

### 2. Recovery Actions
```rust
pub fn recover_orphan(
    inode_num: InodeNumber,
    fs: &mut OpenFs,
    cx: &Cx,
) -> Result<OrphanAction> {
    let inode = fs.read_inode(inode_num)?;
    
    if inode.link_count == 0 {
        // Deleted file, reclaim blocks and inode
        reclaim_inode_blocks(inode_num, fs, cx)?;
        free_inode(inode_num, fs, cx)?;
        Ok(OrphanAction::Deleted)
    } else {
        // Truncated file, fix size
        truncate_to_size(inode_num, inode.size, fs, cx)?;
        Ok(OrphanAction::Truncated)
    }
}
```

### 3. Mount Integration
- Process orphan list before journal replay
- Clear orphan list after processing
- Log actions taken

### 4. Error Handling
- Invalid orphan inode -> log and skip
- Circular list -> detect and break
- I/O error -> mount read-only

## TESTS
1. Unit: Parse orphan list from superblock
2. Integration: Orphan with link_count=0 deleted
3. Integration: Orphan with link_count>0 truncated
4. Integration: Corrupt orphan list handled gracefully

## LOGGING
- Orphan list head (debug)
- Each orphan processed (info): inode, action
- Orphan list cleared (info)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. All orphans processed on mount
2. Blocks correctly reclaimed
3. No orphan references remain
4.
